Alright, let's talk a little bit about how to optimize your environment. So, to do this I'd like to share a concept known as choice architecture. And one of the best examples of choice architecture comes from a team of researchers at Harvard that were looking at Massachusetts General Hospital and the eating habits of different patients and visitors in the hospital cafeteria. So this is how it started. The group went into the cafeteria, analyzed what people were typically eating and drinking, and then thought about how can we change this environment in a way that will shift people's habits in a good way without even talking to them, without trying to motivate them, without asking them to have increased willpower to set a goal. Can we change people's behavior automatically without even discussing it with them? So this is what they did. You can imagine walking into the hospital cafeteria and all around the side are these different buffet lines with food and different places to get something to eat. Then, in the center of the room, there are two cash registers, and next to the cash registers there are these refrigerators. And the refrigerators are filled with a variety of different drink options, food and water and soda. And on the side of the room there's a small refrigerator that has water in it, but that's the only place where water is available. So at this point the researchers decided we're going to put water throughout the entire cafeteria. So they kept the soda available, but they just added water to every refrigerator and then also some additional areas throughout the cafeteria. And what they found was quite interesting. Over the next six months sales of water increased substantially, 26%, and sales of soda decreased by 11%. Now if you were to go up to any individual sitting in the cafeteria and ask them, why did you order this soda or why did you get this water? Everybody would be able to come up with a reason. They'd say, well this is what I wanted to drink or I really like Coke or whatever their reason may be. But in fact many of them bought it not because they had a good reason or because they actively sought it out, but simply because it was presented to them as an option. And this is the punchline of many of our habits and behaviors throughout daily life. We make choices based on the options that are presented to us and not based on what we actually want to achieve or what we consciously seek out. And this idea is known as choice architecture, that if we can change the architecture the way that options are presented to us each day, that we'll actually make different choices. And there are a variety of researchers and scientists who have taken advantage of this, marketers especially, in a business sense. So for example if you walk into a grocery store, items on the end of the aisles, on the end caps, those get purchased more than things that are in the middle of the aisle. So very prominent things are featured on the end of the aisles. Items that are on shelves at eye level get purchased more than things that are down by your feet. So often more profit maximizing products will be put at eye level. Now you and I can take advantage of these discoveries in a different way. We can optimize our home environment or our work spaces, any place that's under our control, to present ourselves with better options in spaces that are more visible, like by putting healthier foods on shelves at eye level in our refrigerators or in our pantries, and by making more unhealthy options less visible. So tucking away unhealthy foods in the corner, in the bottom, or down by our feet in the pantry or in the fridge. But there are a variety of other examples that we can use too, right? I've been talking about food mostly, but choice architecture extends to many of our habits throughout life. You know, if you walk into pretty much any living room, all the couches and chairs face the television. So what is that room designed to get people to do? It's designed to get people to watch TV. And if we wonder why we're watching shows so mindlessly or watching for hours each night, it's often because that's what the environment promotes. So there are a variety of choices you can make here. You know, you could do something simple, like take the remote and take it off the table and tuck it inside of a drawer so that, you know, you don't see it as much. You could take the TV and put it inside a cabinet and close the door so that you're not stimulated to watch it. You could also do something more extreme, like unplug the television, put it in a closet, and then when you really want to watch something like a big game on the weekend or your favorite show, you know, that one week night, take it back out and plug it in. The point here is the same. How can you optimize the environment to make the good behaviors easier and the bad behaviors harder? A couple of ways that you can do this is through a strategy that BJ Fogg, Professor at Stanford University, calls designing for laziness. And he describes it as follows. So Fogg likes popcorn. He enjoys eating it, but he just didn't want to eat as much of it. And so he took the popcorn out of his pantry, walked down the hallway to his garage, and put the popcorn on the highest shelf in the garage. Now, if he really wants it, he can just go out there and get it, right? But if he's designing for his lazy decision, for his default action, he's not going to go out and get the popcorn. And that's the core idea here. How can you design your environment so that your lazy choice, the choice you make when you're exhausted, the choice you make when, you know, you're just tired and don't want to make a decision is actually a better choice that you're presented with a good option by default rather than a bad option. And so there are a variety of ways you can do this. You can do it not just through the examples I've given so far, but also by designing your future actions. So for example, say you want to eat less. Well, you could just buy smaller plates. Research has shown that the average size of a plate has increased by about 22% since the 1970s, and that on average, we fill over 95% of our plate and eat over 95% of what we serve ourselves. So by default, if we make the plate size smaller, we'll serve ourselves less and we eat less. And that's a simple way to design your future actions for eating habits. But you can also do this for other habits like take productivity habits, for example. If you have trouble powering off and going to bed at night, whether that's because you're watching Netflix too late or staying up to work too long, you could use an outlet timer, which you can buy on Amazon for $5 or $10 and then set a time for that outlet to be killed at the end of the night. So at 10pm, for example, the power shuts off to that outlet. TV turns off, you go to bed. You can also set up things like there's one called HomeKit that turns off your lights automatically at a given time throughout at each night. So there are a variety of ways to automate this, but the idea is the same. How do you structure your environment to make the choices automated for yourself or to present yourself with better options rather than trying to fight through your environment to make a positive change? So now I'd like you to click on the worksheet below this video. Think about the habit that you want to build and then write out a few examples of how you could design your environment to promote that habit, to make it easier to put fewer steps between you and the good behavior and more steps between you and the items that distract you from accomplishing that habit. So go ahead and do that now. I'll see you in the next lesson.
